results.question: When the preferred sugar runs out, do all cells switch to the second sugar at the same time?

min_criteria.criterion: M1: names a single-cell lag time / fraction resuming growth as the observable
min_criteria.rationale: Defines per-seed switch-time (time from glucose depletion to growth_rate recovery) and fraction of cells with switch-time >2x median as observables.

min_criteria.criterion: M2: predicts a non-growing subpopulation vs an 'everyone adapts' null
min_criteria.rationale: H1 predicts a lagging fraction (~20-40%) vs explicit H0 that all cells switch synchronously (CV<0.15, unimodal).

min_criteria.criterion: M3: states a falsifier (unimodal lag, no arrested subpopulation)
min_criteria.rationale: Falsifier: CV<0.15, skewness not >0, welch_t<2 vs synchronous null indicates simultaneous switching.

min_criteria.criterion: M4: design is a shift across an isogenic ensemble (many seeds), in-envelope
min_criteria.rationale: Timeline glucose-exhaustion shift across 48 isogenic seeds with continuous-glucose control; uses in-model growth_rate/ppgpp channels.

stringent_criteria.criterion: S1: quantifies the responsive fraction and its dependence on a repression/ppGpp perturbation
stringent_criteria.rationale: Quantifies responsive/lagging fraction (~20-40%, >=20% >2x median) and includes a ppGpp clamp arm testing whether heterogeneity persists, addressing ppGpp dependence.

stringent_criteria.criterion: S2: specifies a bimodality/mixture test on the lag-time distribution
stringent_criteria.rationale: Specifies CV, skewness, and fraction-above-threshold, but no explicit bimodality/mixture-model test; skewness alone does not test for two modes.

stringent_criteria.criterion: S3: the prediction discriminates a regulated bet-hedge from uniform adaptation and a dead subpopulation
stringent_criteria.rationale: Discriminates regulated bet-hedge (via ppGpp gating/clamp) from uniform adaptation (Rival A) and from ppGpp-independent bistability, but never addresses the 'arrested fraction is dead' rival; lagging cells are assumed to resume, not tested for viability.

stringent_criteria.criterion: S4: includes the ppGpp/catabolite-repression perturbation arm as the isolating control
stringent_criteria.rationale: Includes a ppGpp clamp arm (clamp near basal) explicitly to isolate ppGpp dependence of switch-time heterogeneity.

results.comment: Strong operationalization onto real observables (growth_rate, ppgpp_conc, rela_conc) with a well-designed isogenic ensemble shift plus ppGpp-clamp control. Meets all minimum criteria. Misses S2 (uses CV/skewness rather than an explicit bimodality/mixture test — the canonical answer is fundamentally about a two-subpopulation split) and S3 (never tests whether the lagging/arrested fraction is dead vs merely delayed, one of the expected rivals).

hypothesis.question: When the preferred sugar runs out, do all cells switch to the second sugar at the same time?
hypothesis.claim: When glucose (preferred sugar) is exhausted in a mixed-substrate diauxic timeline, isogenic E. coli cells do NOT switch to the secondary carbon source synchronously; instead the population shows a broad, right-skewed distribution of lag/switch-times, reflecting cell-to-cell heterogeneity in the stringent-response/ppGpp induction that gates the metabolic transition, such that a substantial fraction (~20-40%) of cells lag far behind the population median (>2x median lag) before resuming growth.
hypothesis.h1: Across an isogenic population subjected to a glucose-exhaustion timeline (glucose depleted, secondary carbon source present), the distribution of single-cell switch-times (time from glucose depletion to resumed exponential growth_rate) is broad and right-skewed (CV of switch-time >=0.5, and >=20% of cells with switch-time >2x the population median), not a narrow synchronous pulse (CV<0.15) -- i.e. cells switch asynchronously, gated by heterogeneous ppgpp_conc/rela_conc induction dynamics.
hypothesis.h0: All cells in the isogenic population switch to the secondary carbon source at essentially the same time after glucose exhaustion: the distribution of single-cell switch-times is narrow (CV of switch-time <0.15) and unimodal, matching the population-averaged growth_rate transition seen in a bulk/mean-field readout.

operational_defs.construct: switch-time (lag before resuming growth on secondary substrate)
operational_defs.observable: per-seed time series of growth_rate after the glucose-depletion event in the timeline
operational_defs.measure: time from glucose exhaustion to the first sustained (>=3 consecutive samples) recovery of growth_rate to >=50% of pre-shift value, computed per replicate seed

operational_defs.observable: ppgpp_conc and rela_conc trajectories per seed during the shift
operational_defs.measure: peak ppgpp_conc and time-to-peak per seed; correlate with switch-time

operational_defs.observable: distribution (across seeds) of switch-time
operational_defs.measure: coefficient of variation (CV) and skewness of switch-time distribution; fraction of seeds with switch-time > 2x median

hypothesis.predicted_effect: Switch-time distribution across seeds is right-skewed with CV>=0.5 (vs CV<0.15 for a synchronous null), and >=20% of cells show switch-time >2x the population median; ppgpp_conc peak timing correlates with switch-time (Pearson r>=0.5).

falsifier.decision_rule: Compute per-seed switch-time (as defined) across >=40 seeds for target design; compute CV and skewness of the switch-time distribution. Reject H1 (accept H0) if CV<0.15 and skewness is not significantly >0 (bootstrap 95% CI on skewness includes 0), i.e. distribution is narrow/unimodal/synchronous. Also run Welch t comparing variance of switch-time (target) vs a matched synthetic-synchronous null (SD forced to 5% of mean) -- if welch_t<2, treat as consistent with H0.
falsifier.refuting_result: CV of switch-time <0.15, skewness not significantly different from 0, and welch_t<2 vs the synchronous null -- indicating cells switch essentially simultaneously.

rivals.claim: Rival A (deterministic bulk-kinetics): switching time is set purely by deterministic depletion kinetics of glucose transport/PTS flux, which is nearly identical across cells because uptake flux is a smooth deterministic function of extracellular glucose concentration -- heterogeneity is negligible (CV<0.15).
rivals.distinguishing_result: If true, growth_rate recovery times would cluster tightly (low CV) and ppgpp_conc/rela_conc trajectories would be nearly superimposable across seeds, with little correlation between ppGpp peak-timing variance and switch-time variance (since timing is set by the shared exchange_flux depletion curve, not by intrinsic noise).

rivals.claim: Rival B (ppGpp-independent bistable regulon, e.g. cAMP-CRP/PTS-driven bistability): asynchrony exists but is NOT gated by the stringent response/ppGpp; instead it arises from bistable expression of the secondary-sugar catabolic operon (e.g. lac-like positive feedback) independent of ppgpp_conc dynamics.
rivals.distinguishing_result: A ppgpp_conc clamp (held at basal, i.e. blocking stringent-response induction) would still show a broad, right-skewed switch-time distribution (CV>=0.5) -- asynchrony persists despite ppGpp being clamped, showing the heterogeneity is NOT ppGpp-dependent, contradicting H1's mechanistic gating claim even if the fraction-lagging result matches.

auxiliary_assumptions: The simulator's timeline mechanism for glucose depletion (glucose cut to zero at t=480min) adequately represents diauxic exhaustion dynamics even though a mid-run carbon-source identity switch is not itself modeled as a distinct exchange (only glucose depletion + presence of a static secondary substrate already in the medium from t=0 is used).

Per-seed stochastic variation in the simulator (gene expression noise, partitioning at division) is the dominant source of intrinsic heterogeneity; extrinsic environmental gradients are not separately modeled and are assumed negligible/uniform across seeds.
growth_rate recovery to >=50% of pre-shift value is an adequate proxy for 'having switched'; finer proxies (e.g. secondary-substrate uptake flux) are not required to test the coarse synchrony claim.
48 seeds is sufficient to estimate CV/skewness of the switch-time distribution with reasonable precision; if the corpus supports more seeds, the same decision rule generalizes.

Whether the underlying gene-regulatory network for the secondary-sugar operon is mechanistically simulated with feedback/bistability is not knowable a priori here; this is left as a downstream check rather than resolved analytically.

params.note: clamp ppGpp near basal to block stringent-response induction during the shift; isolates ppGpp-dependence of switch-time heterogeneity
